title: Pod Lifecycle States
description:
## What does the `CrashLoopBackOff` status mean for a Pod?

You observe the following when running `kubectl get pods`:

```
NAME          READY   STATUS             RESTARTS   AGE
my-app-xyz    0/1     CrashLoopBackOff   5          3m
```

What is the Kubernetes control plane communicating with this status?

options.text: The pod image could not be pulled from the container registry
The container starts, crashes, and Kubernetes keeps restarting it with exponential backoff delay
The pod is waiting for a PersistentVolume to become available
The pod has been evicted from the node due to resource pressure

explanation: `CrashLoopBackOff` means the container is repeatedly crashing after startup. Kubernetes restarts it automatically but introduces increasing delays (backoff) between attempts to avoid overwhelming the system. Common causes include a bad entrypoint command, missing environment variables, or application errors on startup.

hints.title: Interpreting pod status
hints.body: Use `kubectl describe pod <name>` to see the Events section — it shows exactly why the container is failing.
hints.command: kubectl describe pod my-app-xyz

hints.title: Reading container logs
hints.body: Even a crashed container leaves logs behind. Use `--previous` to read the logs from the last crash.
hints.command: kubectl logs my-app-xyz --previous

title: Deploy and Expose Nginx
description:
## Deploy and Expose an Nginx Application

The platform team needs a simple web server running in the cluster.

**Your tasks:**
1. Create a **Deployment** named `webserver` in the `default` namespace
2. Use the image `nginx:1.25`
3. Set replica count to **2**
4. Expose it via a **ClusterIP Service** named `webserver-svc` on port **80**

> 💡 Tip: You can use `kubectl create` for both resources imperatively, or write YAML manifests.

hints.title: Create the Deployment
hints.body: Use `kubectl create deployment` with the `--image` and `--replicas` flags.
hints.command: kubectl create deployment webserver --image=nginx:1.25 --replicas=2

hints.title: Expose the Deployment
hints.body: Use `kubectl expose` to create a ClusterIP service targeting port 80.
hints.command: kubectl expose deployment webserver --name=webserver-svc --port=80 --target-port=80

hints.title: Verify
hints.body: Check that both resources are up and the service endpoints are populated.
hints.command: kubectl get deployment webserver && kubectl get svc webserver-svc && kubectl get endpoints webserver-svc

validation.description: Checks that the deployment exists with 2 replicas, uses the correct image, and the service exists targeting port 80.

title: Mount ConfigMap as Volume
description:
## Inject Configuration via a ConfigMap Volume

An application reads its configuration from files on disk at `/etc/app-config/`.

**Your tasks:**
1. Create a **ConfigMap** named `app-config` in the `default` namespace with the following key-value pairs:
   - `environment`: `production`
   - `log_level`: `warn`
   - `max_connections`: `200`
2. Create a **Pod** named `config-reader` using image `busybox:1.36`
3. Mount the ConfigMap as a **volume** at `/etc/app-config` inside the container
4. The pod should run the command: `sleep 3600`

hints.title: Create the ConfigMap
hints.body: Use `--from-literal` for each key-value pair.
hints.command: kubectl create configmap app-config --from-literal=environment=production --from-literal=log_level=warn --from-literal=max_connections=200

hints.title: Write the Pod manifest
hints.body: Use a heredoc piped directly to kubectl apply -f - to avoid writing to a temp file.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: config-reader
spec:
  containers:
  - name: reader
    image: busybox:1.36
    command: ["sleep", "3600"]
    volumeMounts:
    - name: config-vol
      mountPath: /etc/app-config
  volumes:
  - name: config-vol
    configMap:
      name: app-config
EOF

hints.title: Apply and verify
hints.body: Apply the manifest and confirm files are visible inside the container.
hints.command: kubectl apply -f /tmp/config-reader.yaml && kubectl exec config-reader -- ls /etc/app-config

validation.description: Verifies ConfigMap exists with correct keys and the pod mounts it at the right path.

title: RBAC: Create Role and RoleBinding
description:
## Restrict Namespace Access with RBAC

A CI/CD service account needs read-only access to pods and deployments in the `staging` namespace.

**Your tasks:**
1. Create namespace `staging`
2. Create a **ServiceAccount** named `ci-reader` in the `staging` namespace
3. Create a **Role** named `read-workloads` in `staging` that allows `get`, `list`, `watch` on `pods` and `deployments`
4. Create a **RoleBinding** named `ci-reader-binding` that binds `read-workloads` to the `ci-reader` ServiceAccount
5. Verify the ServiceAccount **can** list pods but **cannot** create them

hints.title: Create namespace and service account
hints.body: Create these first before the role, as the RoleBinding references both.
hints.command:
kubectl create namespace staging
kubectl create serviceaccount ci-reader -n staging

hints.title: Create the Role
hints.body: Use `kubectl create role` with multiple `--verb` and `--resource` flags.
hints.command:
kubectl create role read-workloads \
  --verb=get,list,watch \
  --resource=pods,deployments \
  -n staging

hints.title: Bind the Role
hints.body: RoleBinding ties a Role to a subject. ServiceAccount subjects need namespace-qualified names.
hints.command:
kubectl create rolebinding ci-reader-binding \
  --role=read-workloads \
  --serviceaccount=staging:ci-reader \
  -n staging

hints.title: Verify permissions with auth can-i
hints.body: Use `--as` to impersonate the service account and test its permissions.
hints.command:
kubectl auth can-i list pods --as=system:serviceaccount:staging:ci-reader -n staging
kubectl auth can-i create pods --as=system:serviceaccount:staging:ci-reader -n staging

validation.description: Validates the full RBAC chain: namespace, SA, role, rolebinding, and effective permissions.

commands.command: kubectl get serviceaccount ci-reader -n staging -o jsonpath='{.metadata.name}'
kubectl get role read-workloads -n staging -o jsonpath='{.metadata.name}'
kubectl get rolebinding ci-reader-binding -n staging -o jsonpath='{.metadata.name}'
kubectl auth can-i list pods --as=system:serviceaccount:staging:ci-reader -n staging
kubectl auth can-i create pods --as=system:serviceaccount:staging:ci-reader -n staging

title: Debug a Failing Deployment
description:
## Fix the Broken Deployment

A deployment has been pre-created in the `debug` namespace but its pods are not running.

**Your task:** Identify the problem and fix it so that the deployment has **all 3 pods in Ready state**.

> Start by describing the deployment and its pods to find clues about what's wrong.

hints.title: Inspect the deployment
hints.body: Start by listing pods in the debug namespace and describing the failing ones.
hints.command:
kubectl get pods -n debug
kubectl describe pod -n debug -l app=broken-app

hints.title: Check events
hints.body: The Events section in `kubectl describe` usually tells you exactly what's wrong — look for ImagePullBackOff, OOMKilled, or probe failures.
hints.command: kubectl describe deployment broken-app -n debug

hints.title: Fix the image tag
hints.body: If the image tag doesn't exist, update it to a valid one using `kubectl set image`.
hints.command: kubectl set image deployment/broken-app app=nginx:1.25 -n debug

validation.description: Checks that the broken-app deployment in the debug namespace has 3 ready replicas.

commands.command: kubectl get deployment broken-app -n debug -o jsonpath='{.status.readyReplicas}' 2>/dev/null | grep -v '^$' || echo 0
kubectl get pods -n debug --no-headers 2>/dev/null | awk '{print $3}' | grep -c 'ImagePullBackOff\|ErrImagePull' || true

title: Persistent Storage with PV and PVC
description:
## Attach Persistent Storage to a Pod

A stateful application needs data to survive pod restarts.

**Your tasks:**
1. Create a **PersistentVolumeClaim** named `local-pvc` in the `default` namespace requesting `200Mi` with:
   - Access mode: `ReadWriteOnce`
   - StorageClass: `local-path` (available in this cluster via k3s)
2. Create a **Pod** named `storage-pod` using image `nginx:1.25` that mounts the PVC at `/data`
3. Write a file inside the pod at `/data/hello.txt` with content `hello-k8s`

hints.title: Create the PVC
hints.body: Use storageClassName: local-path to use the k3s built-in dynamic provisioner. No PV needs to be created manually.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: local-pvc
spec:
  accessModes: [ReadWriteOnce]
  storageClassName: local-path
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 200Mi
EOF

hints.title: Create the Pod with PVC mount
hints.body: Reference the PVC by name under volumes and mount it in the container.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: storage-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:1.25
    volumeMounts:
    - name: data
      mountPath: /data
  volumes:
  - name: data
    persistentVolumeClaim:
      claimName: local-pvc
EOF

hints.title: Write file into the pod
hints.body: Use kubectl exec to write a file into the mounted volume.
hints.command: kubectl exec storage-pod -- sh -c 'echo hello-k8s > /data/hello.txt'

validation.description: Validates PV, PVC binding, pod running, and file written to the volume.

title: Isolate Traffic with NetworkPolicy
description:
## Implement Network Isolation

A pre-created `database` pod in the `netpol` namespace must only accept traffic from pods labeled `role=backend`.

**Your tasks:**
1. Create a **NetworkPolicy** named `db-isolate` in the `netpol` namespace that:
   - Targets pods with label `app=database`
   - Allows **ingress** only from pods with label `role=backend` in the **same namespace**
   - Denies all other ingress traffic
2. Verify the policy is applied correctly

> The `database` and `backend` pods are pre-created for you.

hints.title: Understand NetworkPolicy selectors
hints.body: A NetworkPolicy uses `podSelector` to pick which pods it applies to, and `ingress.from` to define allowed sources.
hints.command: kubectl explain networkpolicy.spec.ingress.from

hints.title: Write the NetworkPolicy
hints.body: An empty ingress rule (no `from`) denies everything. Specifying a `from` allows only those sources.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: db-isolate
  namespace: netpol
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: database
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  ingress:
  - from:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          role: backend
EOF

hints.title: Verify the policy
hints.body: List NetworkPolicies to confirm it was created.
hints.command: kubectl get networkpolicy db-isolate -n netpol -o yaml

validation.description: Verifies the NetworkPolicy exists and correctly targets the database pod.

commands.command: kubectl get networkpolicy db-isolate -n netpol -o jsonpath='{.metadata.name}'

commands.description: Policy targets pods with label app=database

commands.command: kubectl get networkpolicy db-isolate -n netpol -o jsonpath='{.spec.policyTypes[0]}'

title: Kubernetes Service Types
description:
## Choose the Right Service Type

You have a microservice running inside the cluster that needs to be reachable **from the internet** (external traffic). The cluster is hosted on a cloud provider (AWS/GCP/Azure).

Which **Service type** should you use?

options.text: ClusterIP — the default service type, only reachable within the cluster
NodePort — opens a port on every node, accessible from outside if the node IP is reachable
LoadBalancer — provisions a cloud load balancer and assigns an external IP automatically
ExternalName — maps the service to a DNS name, not suitable for external ingress

explanation: `LoadBalancer` is the right choice for production external traffic on cloud-hosted clusters. It provisions a cloud provider load balancer (like an AWS ALB or GCP L4 LB) and assigns an external IP. `NodePort` works in theory but requires knowing node IPs and is not production-grade. `ClusterIP` is internal-only. `ExternalName` is for mapping services to external DNS names.

hints.title: Recall Service types
hints.body: There are 4 service types: ClusterIP (internal), NodePort (node-level), LoadBalancer (cloud LB), ExternalName (DNS alias). Each builds on the previous in terms of exposure level.
hints.command: kubectl explain service.spec.type

title: Working with Namespaces
description:
## Working with Namespaces

Namespaces provide a mechanism for isolating groups of resources within a single cluster.

**Your task:**

1. Create a namespace called `team-alpha`
2. Deploy a `nginx:alpine` Deployment named `web` with **2 replicas** inside the `team-alpha` namespace

**Verify your work:**
```bash
kubectl get namespace team-alpha
kubectl get deployment web -n team-alpha
```

hints.title: Create a namespace
hints.body: Use `kubectl create namespace <name>` to create a new namespace.
hints.command: kubectl create namespace team-alpha

hints.title: Deploy into a specific namespace
hints.body: Use the `-n` or `--namespace` flag with `kubectl create deployment`. Alternatively use `kubectl apply -f` with `namespace` set in the manifest metadata.
hints.command: kubectl create deployment web --image=nginx:alpine --replicas=2 -n team-alpha

commands.command: kubectl get deployment web -n team-alpha -o jsonpath='{.metadata.name}'
kubectl get deployment web -n team-alpha -o jsonpath='{.spec.replicas}'

title: Labels and Selectors
description:
## Labels and Selectors

You run the following command:

```bash
kubectl get pods -l env=prod,tier=frontend
```

What does this command return?

options.text: Pods that have BOTH `env=prod` AND `tier=frontend` labels
Pods that have either `env=prod` OR `tier=frontend` labels
Pods that do NOT have `env=prod` or `tier=frontend` labels
All pods, sorted by the `env` and `tier` label values

explanation: When you specify multiple label selector expressions separated by commas, Kubernetes applies a logical **AND** — all conditions must be true. Only pods that have both `env=prod` **and** `tier=frontend` are returned. Use `-l 'env in (prod,staging)'` for OR-style matching.

hints.title: How comma-separated selectors work
hints.body: The `-l` flag accepts a comma-separated list of `key=value` expressions. Multiple expressions are ANDed together — all must match for a pod to be included in the result.
hints.command: kubectl get pods -l env=prod,tier=frontend

title: Resource Requests and Limits
description:
## Resource Requests and Limits

Proper resource management prevents noisy-neighbour issues and enables the Kubernetes scheduler to make good placement decisions.

**Your task:**

Create a Pod named `limited-pod` using the `nginx:alpine` image with the following resource configuration:

| | CPU | Memory |
|---|---|---|
| **Request** | `100m` | `64Mi` |
| **Limit** | `200m` | `128Mi` |

```bash
# Tip: write a manifest and apply it
kubectl apply -f limited-pod.yaml
```

hints.title: Pod manifest with resources
hints.body: Add a `resources` block under `spec.containers[].resources` with `requests` and `limits` sub-keys.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: limited-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:alpine
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: "100m"
        memory: "64Mi"
      limits:
        cpu: "200m"
        memory: "128Mi"
EOF

title: Creating and Using Secrets
description:
## Creating and Using Secrets

Secrets let you store sensitive data (passwords, tokens, keys) separately from your pod specs.

**Your task:**

1. Create a Secret named `app-secret` with the key `api-key` and value `supersecret`
2. Create a Pod named `secret-reader` using `busybox:1.36` that:
   - Mounts the secret key `api-key` as an environment variable named `API_KEY`
   - Runs: `sleep 3600`

**Verify:**
```bash
kubectl exec secret-reader -- env | grep API_KEY
```

hints.title: Create the Secret
hints.body: Use `kubectl create secret generic` with `--from-literal`.
hints.command: kubectl create secret generic app-secret --from-literal=api-key=supersecret

hints.title: Reference secret in a Pod env var
hints.body: Use `env[].valueFrom.secretKeyRef` in the container spec to map a secret key to an env var.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secret-reader
spec:
  containers:
  - name: reader
    image: busybox:1.36
    command: ["sleep","3600"]
    env:
    - name: API_KEY
      valueFrom:
        secretKeyRef:
          name: app-secret
          key: api-key
EOF

commands.description: Pod references the secret via env var

title: Configure a Liveness Probe
description:
## Configure a Liveness Probe

Kubernetes uses **liveness probes** to know when to restart a container. If a container's liveness probe fails repeatedly, the kubelet kills the container and the pod's restart policy takes effect.

**Your task:**

Create a Deployment named `probed-app` with:
- Image: `nginx:alpine`
- 1 replica
- A **liveness probe** that:
  - Checks `HTTP GET /` on port `80`
  - `initialDelaySeconds: 5`
  - `periodSeconds: 10`

hints.title: Liveness probe structure
hints.body: Add `livenessProbe` under `spec.containers[]`. Use `httpGet` with `path` and `port` fields.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: probed-app
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: probed-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: probed-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: nginx:alpine
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
EOF

commands.description: Liveness probe uses HTTP GET on port 80

title: Jobs vs CronJobs
description:
## Jobs vs CronJobs

A data-processing team needs to run a database backup script **every night at midnight**. Which Kubernetes resource should they use, and why?

options.text: A `Job` — because Jobs run a task until it completes successfully, including on a nightly schedule
A `CronJob` — because it creates Jobs on a time-based schedule (cron syntax) and is designed for recurring tasks
A `Deployment` with `restartPolicy: OnFailure` — deployments keep tasks running on schedule
A `DaemonSet` — because it ensures exactly one backup process per node nightly

explanation: A **CronJob** is the right resource for recurring scheduled tasks. It uses standard cron syntax (e.g. `0 0 * * *` for midnight daily) and creates a new `Job` object at each scheduled time. A plain `Job` runs once to completion — you would need external scheduling to run it nightly. Deployments are for long-running services, and DaemonSets run pods on every node.

hints.title: CronJob syntax
hints.body: CronJobs use standard cron format: `minute hour day-of-month month day-of-week`. For midnight daily: `0 0 * * *`.
hints.command: kubectl explain cronjob.spec.schedule

title: Perform a Rolling Update
description:
## Perform a Rolling Update

A Deployment named `app-v1` is running `nginx:1.24`. Your team needs to upgrade it to `nginx:1.25`.

**Your task:**

Update the image of the `app-v1` deployment to `nginx:1.25` using a rolling update strategy. Ensure all replicas are running the new image.

```bash
# Hint: kubectl set image can update an image in-place
kubectl set image deployment/<name> <container>=<image>
```

hints.title: Set a new image on a Deployment
hints.body: Use `kubectl set image deployment/<name> <container-name>=<new-image>` to trigger a rolling update.
hints.command: kubectl set image deployment/app-v1 nginx=nginx:1.25

hints.title: Check rollout progress
hints.body: Monitor the rollout with `kubectl rollout status`. Once complete, all pods will run the new image.
hints.command: kubectl get deployment app-v1 -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}'

commands.command: kubectl get deployment app-v1 -o jsonpath='{.status.readyReplicas}' 2>/dev/null | grep -v '^$' || echo 0

title: Init Containers Behaviour
description:
## Init Containers Behaviour

A pod spec defines two init containers (`init-db-check` and `init-config`) followed by one main app container (`web`).

Which statement **correctly** describes how Kubernetes runs these containers?

options.text: All three containers start simultaneously; init containers simply have lower priority
Init containers run sequentially to completion before the main `web` container starts
The `web` container starts first, and init containers run as sidecars alongside it
Init containers cannot share volumes with the main container

explanation: Init containers always run **sequentially** and must each exit with a success (exit code 0) before the next one starts. Only after **all** init containers complete successfully does Kubernetes start the main application containers. They can share volumes with main containers, making them ideal for setup tasks like seeding configs, waiting for dependencies, or initialising databases.

hints.title: Init container execution order
hints.body: Check `kubectl explain pod.spec.initContainers`. Each init container runs to completion before the next begins, and all must succeed before app containers start.
hints.command: kubectl explain pod.spec.initContainers

title: Environment Variables from ConfigMap
description:
## Environment Variables from ConfigMap

ConfigMaps can inject configuration as environment variables into pods, keeping application images generic and portable.

**Your task:**

1. Create a ConfigMap named `app-env` with two keys:
   - `LOG_LEVEL=debug`
   - `APP_PORT=8080`

2. Create a Pod named `env-pod` using `busybox:1.36` with command `sleep 3600` that loads **all keys** from `app-env` as environment variables using `envFrom`.

**Verify:**
```bash
kubectl exec env-pod -- env | grep -E 'LOG_LEVEL|APP_PORT'
```

hints.title: Create the ConfigMap
hints.body: Use `kubectl create configmap` with multiple `--from-literal` flags.
hints.command: kubectl create configmap app-env --from-literal=LOG_LEVEL=debug --from-literal=APP_PORT=8080

hints.title: Load all ConfigMap keys via envFrom
hints.body: Use `envFrom` with `configMapRef` to load all keys at once — simpler than mapping each key individually.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: env-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: busybox:1.36
    command: ["sleep","3600"]
    envFrom:
    - configMapRef:
        name: app-env
EOF

title: Understanding DaemonSets
description:
## Understanding DaemonSets

Your team wants to deploy a **log collector agent** that must run on **every node** in the cluster, including any nodes added in the future.

Which Kubernetes resource is the best fit?

options.text: A `Deployment` with `replicas` set to the number of nodes
A `StatefulSet` with `replicas` matching the node count
A `DaemonSet`, which automatically places one pod per node and adapts as nodes join or leave
A `CronJob` that runs `kubectl create pod` on each node every minute

explanation: A **DaemonSet** guarantees that exactly one copy of a pod runs on every (or selected) node. As nodes are added to the cluster, the DaemonSet controller automatically schedules the pod on them; when nodes are removed, the pods are garbage-collected. Classic use cases: log shippers (Fluentd, Filebeat), monitoring agents (Prometheus Node Exporter), and CNI plugins. A Deployment with fixed replicas does NOT guarantee one-pod-per-node coverage.

hints.title: When to use a DaemonSet
hints.body: DaemonSets are ideal for cluster-level infrastructure: log collection, metrics agents, node monitoring, and CNI/CSI plugins that need to run on every node.
hints.command: kubectl explain daemonset.spec

title: Scaling a Deployment
description:
## Scaling a Deployment

A deployment named `webapp` is currently running with **1 replica**. Traffic has increased and you need to scale it up.

**Your task:**

Scale the `webapp` deployment to **4 replicas** and verify all pods become Ready.

```bash
# You can use the imperative command
kubectl scale deployment webapp --replicas=4
# Or edit the manifest
kubectl edit deployment webapp
```

hints.title: Scale imperatively
hints.body: `kubectl scale` is the quickest way to change replica count without editing a YAML file.
hints.command: kubectl scale deployment webapp --replicas=4

hints.title: Verify the scale
hints.body: Check READY column in `kubectl get deployment webapp` — it should show 4/4.
hints.command: kubectl get deployment webapp

commands.command: kubectl get deployment webapp -o jsonpath='{.status.readyReplicas}' 2>/dev/null | grep -v '^$' || echo 0

title: Essential kubectl Commands
description:
## Essential kubectl Commands

You need to quickly view the **logs** of a container named `api` inside a pod named `backend-7d9f`. The pod has multiple containers.

Which command is correct?

options.text: `kubectl describe pod backend-7d9f`
`kubectl logs backend-7d9f`
`kubectl logs backend-7d9f -c api`
`kubectl exec backend-7d9f -- cat /var/log/api.log`

explanation: When a pod has **multiple containers**, you must specify which container's logs you want using the `-c <container-name>` flag: `kubectl logs <pod-name> -c <container-name>`. Without `-c`, kubectl returns an error if the pod has more than one container. `kubectl describe` shows metadata and events, not live logs. `kubectl exec` can work but is cumbersome and container-runtime dependent.

hints.title: kubectl logs flags
hints.body: Key flags: `-c` (container name for multi-container pods), `-f` (follow/stream), `--previous` (crashed container logs), `--since=1h` (time filter).
hints.command: kubectl logs --help | head -30

title: ClusterRole and ClusterRoleBinding
description:
## ClusterRole and ClusterRoleBinding

RBAC **ClusterRoles** grant permissions cluster-wide (across all namespaces), unlike Roles which are namespace-scoped.

**Your task:**

1. Create a **ClusterRole** named `pod-reader` that allows `get`, `list`, `watch` on `pods`
2. Create a **ClusterRoleBinding** named `pod-reader-binding` that binds `pod-reader` to the ServiceAccount `default` in the `default` namespace

```bash
kubectl create clusterrole --help
kubectl create clusterrolebinding --help
```

hints.title: Create the ClusterRole
hints.body: Use `kubectl create clusterrole` with `--verb` and `--resource` flags.
hints.command: kubectl create clusterrole pod-reader --verb=get,list,watch --resource=pods

hints.title: Create the ClusterRoleBinding
hints.body: Bind the ClusterRole to a ServiceAccount using `--serviceaccount=namespace:name`.
hints.command: kubectl create clusterrolebinding pod-reader-binding --clusterrole=pod-reader --serviceaccount=default:default

title: Node Labels and nodeSelector
description:
## Node Labels and nodeSelector

Labelling nodes lets you constrain which nodes a pod can be scheduled on, using `nodeSelector` in the pod spec.

**Your task:**

1. Add the label `disk=ssd` to node `k8s-lab`
2. Create a Pod named `ssd-pod` using `nginx:alpine` that uses `nodeSelector` to target nodes with `disk=ssd`

hints.title: Label the node
hints.body: Use `kubectl label node <node-name> key=value`.
hints.command: kubectl label node k8s-lab disk=ssd --overwrite

hints.title: Use nodeSelector in pod spec
hints.body: Add `nodeSelector: disk: ssd` under `spec` in the pod manifest.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: ssd-pod
spec:
  nodeSelector:
    disk: ssd
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:alpine
EOF

teardown_commands.command: kubectl label node k8s-lab disk- 2>/dev/null || true

title: Taints and Tolerations
description:
## Taints and Tolerations

Taints allow a node to **repel** pods. A toleration on a pod allows it to be scheduled onto a tainted node.

The node `k8s-lab` has been tainted with `env=gpu:NoSchedule`.

**Your task:**

Create a Pod named `gpu-pod` using `nginx:alpine` that tolerates the taint `env=gpu:NoSchedule` so it can be scheduled on this node.

```bash
kubectl describe node k8s-lab | grep -A5 Taints
```

hints.title: Toleration structure
hints.body: Add a `tolerations` block under `spec`. Match `key`, `operator`, `value`, and `effect` to the node taint.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: gpu-pod
spec:
  tolerations:
  - key: "env"
    operator: "Equal"
    value: "gpu"
    effect: "NoSchedule"
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:alpine
EOF

setup_commands.command: kubectl taint nodes k8s-lab env=gpu:NoSchedule --overwrite=true 2>/dev/null || true

teardown_commands.command: kubectl taint nodes k8s-lab env:NoSchedule- 2>/dev/null || true

title: etcd Backup and Restore
description:
## etcd Backup and Restore

In the CKA exam, you may need to back up and restore an etcd cluster. Which command correctly creates an etcd snapshot?

```bash
# The etcdctl binary is available on the control-plane node.
# Assume certificates are at /etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/
```

options.text: `etcdctl snapshot save /backup/etcd.db` (no flags needed — etcdctl auto-detects certs)

`ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot save /backup/etcd.db --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key`
`kubectl exec etcd-controlplane -- etcdctl backup --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd`
`etcdctl snapshot save /backup/etcd.db --kubeconfig=/root/.kube/config`

explanation: etcd requires **TLS certificates** and explicit endpoint specification. You must set `ETCDCTL_API=3`, point to the etcd endpoint (`https://127.0.0.1:2379` on control-plane), and provide the CA cert, server cert, and key from `/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/`. To restore: `ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot restore /backup/etcd.db --data-dir=/var/lib/etcd-restored`, then update the etcd static pod manifest to point to the new data dir.

hints.title: etcdctl API version
hints.body: Always set `ETCDCTL_API=3`. etcdctl v2 and v3 APIs are different — the CKA exam uses v3. The certs live at `/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/`.
hints.command: ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status /backup/etcd.db --write-out=table

title: ResourceQuota for a Namespace
description:
## ResourceQuota for a Namespace

ResourceQuotas enforce aggregate resource constraints per namespace, preventing any single team from consuming all cluster resources.

**Your task:**

1. Create a namespace named `team-quota`
2. Create a **ResourceQuota** named `compute-quota` in `team-quota` that limits:
   - `pods`: `5`
   - `requests.cpu`: `"1"`
   - `requests.memory`: `"500Mi"`

hints.title: Create the ResourceQuota
hints.body: Use `kubectl create resourcequota` with `--hard` flag or apply a YAML manifest.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: compute-quota
  namespace: team-quota
spec:
  hard:
    pods: "5"
    requests.cpu: "1"
    requests.memory: "500Mi"
EOF

commands.command: kubectl get resourcequota compute-quota -n team-quota -o jsonpath='{.metadata.name}'
kubectl get resourcequota compute-quota -n team-quota -o jsonpath='{.spec.hard.pods}'
kubectl get resourcequota compute-quota -n team-quota -o jsonpath='{.spec.hard.requests\.cpu}'

title: Rolling Back a Deployment
description:
## Rolling Back a Deployment

A deployment `api-server` was mistakenly updated to a broken image. Your task is to roll it back to the previous working revision.

**Your task:**

Roll back the `api-server` deployment to its previous revision.

```bash
# Check rollout history
kubectl rollout history deployment/api-server
# Rollback
kubectl rollout undo deployment/api-server
```

hints.title: Undo a rollout
hints.body: `kubectl rollout undo deployment/<name>` reverts to the previous revision. Use `--to-revision=N` to go to a specific revision number.
hints.command: kubectl rollout undo deployment/api-server

commands.description: Deployment 'api-server' image rolled back to nginx:1.24

title: Create a CronJob
description:
## Create a CronJob

CronJobs create Jobs on a repeating schedule, perfect for maintenance tasks like log rotation, backups, or report generation.

**Your task:**

Create a CronJob named `date-printer` that:
- Runs **every minute** (`* * * * *`)
- Uses `busybox:1.36`
- Executes: `date`
- Has `successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3`

hints.title: CronJob manifest
hints.body: Use `kubectl create cronjob` or apply a manifest. The schedule uses standard cron syntax.
hints.command: kubectl create cronjob date-printer --image=busybox:1.36 --schedule='* * * * *' -- date

title: Configure a Readiness Probe
description:
## Configure a Readiness Probe

A **readiness probe** tells Kubernetes when a container is ready to accept traffic. Unlike a liveness probe (which restarts containers), a failed readiness probe removes the pod from Service endpoints until it recovers.

**Your task:**

Create a Deployment named `ready-app` with:
- Image: `nginx:alpine`, 2 replicas
- A **readiness probe**: HTTP GET `/` on port `80`, `initialDelaySeconds: 3`, `periodSeconds: 5`

hints.title: Readiness probe YAML
hints.body: Add `readinessProbe` under the container spec — same structure as `livenessProbe` but controls traffic routing, not restarts.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: ready-app
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: ready-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: ready-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: nginx:alpine
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 80
          initialDelaySeconds: 3
          periodSeconds: 5
EOF

title: Expose a Deployment via NodePort
description:
## Expose a Deployment via NodePort

A **NodePort** service exposes an application on a static port on every node, making it accessible outside the cluster without a cloud load balancer.

**Your task:**

A deployment `frontend` is already running. Expose it as a **NodePort** service named `frontend-np` on:
- Service port `80`
- NodePort `30080`
- Target port `80`

hints.title: Create NodePort service
hints.body: Use `kubectl expose` or apply a Service manifest with `type: NodePort` and the `nodePort` field.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: frontend-np
spec:
  type: NodePort
  selector:
    app: frontend
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 80
    nodePort: 30080
EOF

title: Kubernetes DNS Resolution
description:
## Kubernetes DNS Resolution

A pod in namespace `frontend` needs to reach a service named `db-service` in namespace `backend`. Which DNS name correctly resolves to that service from within the cluster?

options.text: `db-service`
`db-service.backend`
`db-service.backend.svc.cluster.local`
`backend.db-service.cluster.local`

explanation: The full Kubernetes DNS format is `<service>.<namespace>.svc.<cluster-domain>`. The default cluster domain is `cluster.local`, so the FQDN is `db-service.backend.svc.cluster.local`. When pods are in the **same namespace**, just `db-service` works. Across namespaces, you need at minimum `db-service.backend` (short form). Option C is the fully-qualified name that always works regardless of the caller's namespace.

hints.title: Kubernetes DNS format
hints.body: CoreDNS resolves: `<svc-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local`. You can verify with: `kubectl exec <pod> -- nslookup <service-name>`.
hints.command: kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -- nslookup db-service.backend.svc.cluster.local

title: StorageClass Reclaim Policies
description:
## StorageClass Reclaim Policies

A PersistentVolumeClaim bound to a PV is deleted. The StorageClass has `reclaimPolicy: Retain`.

What happens to the underlying PersistentVolume?

options.text: The PV is immediately deleted along with all data
The PV remains but enters a `Released` state; the data is preserved and must be manually reclaimed
The PV is automatically rebound to the next PVC that requests the same storage class
The PV is converted to an emptyDir volume attached to the node

explanation: With `reclaimPolicy: Retain`, when a PVC is deleted the PV moves to `Released` state — the data is **preserved** on the storage backend, but the PV is not available for automatic rebinding. An admin must manually reclaim it (delete the PV, clean the storage, recreate the PV). Contrast with `reclaimPolicy: Delete`, which deletes both the PV object and the underlying storage asset automatically.

hints.title: Reclaim policies
hints.body: Three policies exist: `Retain` (manual reclaim), `Delete` (auto-delete storage), `Recycle` (deprecated, basic scrub). Check with: `kubectl get storageclass`.
hints.command: kubectl get storageclass -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}={.reclaimPolicy}{"\n"}{end}'

title: Shared emptyDir Volume Between Containers
description:
## Shared emptyDir Volume

An `emptyDir` volume is created when a Pod is assigned to a node and exists as long as the Pod runs. It is ideal for sharing data between containers in the same pod (e.g., sidecar patterns).

**Your task:**

Create a Pod named `shared-data` with two containers:
1. **writer** (`busybox:1.36`) — writes `hello-kube` to `/shared/message.txt` then sleeps
2. **reader** (`busybox:1.36`) — reads and prints `/shared/message.txt` then sleeps

Both containers mount an `emptyDir` volume at `/shared`.

hints.title: emptyDir shared volume manifest
hints.body: Define a single volume of type `emptyDir: {}` and mount it in both containers under the same path.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: shared-data
spec:
  containers:
  - name: writer
    image: busybox:1.36
    command: ["sh","-c","echo hello-kube > /shared/message.txt && sleep 3600"]
    volumeMounts:
    - name: shared-vol
      mountPath: /shared
  - name: reader
    image: busybox:1.36
    command: ["sh","-c","sleep 5 && cat /shared/message.txt && sleep 3600"]
    volumeMounts:
    - name: shared-vol
      mountPath: /shared
  volumes:
  - name: shared-vol
    emptyDir: {}
EOF

commands.description: Writer container mounts the shared volume

title: Pod Security Context
description:
## Pod Security Context

A security context defines privilege and access control settings for a pod or container.

**Your task:**

Create a Pod named `secure-pod` using `nginx:alpine` with the following security settings:
- `runAsUser: 1000`
- `runAsNonRoot: true`
- `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false`

```bash
# Verify after creation:
kubectl exec secure-pod -- id
```

hints.title: securityContext structure
hints.body: Security context can be set at Pod level (`spec.securityContext`) or container level (`spec.containers[].securityContext`). Container-level settings override pod-level.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:alpine
    securityContext:
      runAsUser: 1000
      runAsNonRoot: true
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
EOF

title: Fix a CrashLoopBackOff Pod
description:
## Fix a CrashLoopBackOff

A deployment `crash-app` is in `CrashLoopBackOff` because it was deployed with a broken command.

**Your task:**

Fix the `crash-app` deployment so all pods run successfully. The container should run `nginx -g 'daemon off;'` (the default nginx command). Update the deployment to remove the broken command override.

```bash
# Check what's wrong:
kubectl describe deployment crash-app
kubectl logs -l app=crash-app
```

hints.title: Remove the broken command
hints.body: Use `kubectl patch` or `kubectl edit deployment crash-app`. Remove the `command` override so nginx uses its default entrypoint.
hints.command: kubectl patch deployment crash-app --type=json -p='[{"op":"remove","path":"/spec/template/spec/containers/0/command"}]'

hints.title: Alternative: kubectl set
hints.body: You can also edit the deployment directly and delete the command field.
hints.command: kubectl edit deployment crash-app

commands.command: kubectl get deployment crash-app -o jsonpath='{.status.readyReplicas}' 2>/dev/null | grep -v '^$' || echo 0

title: Fix a Broken Service Selector
description:
## Fix a Broken Service Selector

A service `my-svc` has been created but no traffic reaches the pods. The pods are labelled `app=backend`, but the service selector is misconfigured.

**Your task:**

Fix the `my-svc` service selector so it correctly targets the `backend` pods.

```bash
# Investigate:
kubectl describe service my-svc
kubectl get endpoints my-svc
kubectl get pods --show-labels
```

hints.title: Check endpoints
hints.body: If `kubectl get endpoints my-svc` shows `<none>`, the selector doesn't match any pods. Compare service selector with pod labels.
hints.command: kubectl get endpoints my-svc

hints.title: Patch the service selector
hints.body: Use `kubectl patch` to fix the selector.
hints.command: kubectl patch service my-svc --type=json -p='[{"op":"replace","path":"/spec/selector/app","value":"backend"}]'

setup_commands.command:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: my-svc
spec:
  selector:
    app: wrong-label
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 80
EOF

commands.command: kubectl get endpoints my-svc -o jsonpath='{.subsets[0].addresses[0].ip}' 2>/dev/null | grep -v '^$' || echo empty

title: Create an Ingress Resource
description:
## Create an Ingress Resource

Ingress exposes HTTP/HTTPS routes from outside the cluster to services within it. k3s includes Traefik as its default Ingress controller.

**Your task:**

A deployment `web-app` and service `web-svc` (port 80) already exist. Create an **Ingress** named `web-ingress` that:
- Routes traffic for host `app.lab.local`
- Path `/` → service `web-svc` on port `80`
- Uses `pathType: Prefix`

hints.title: Ingress manifest
hints.body: Use `networking.k8s.io/v1` for the Ingress API. Define `spec.rules[].host` and `spec.rules[].http.paths[]`.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: web-ingress
spec:
  rules:
  - host: app.lab.local
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: web-svc
            port:
              number: 80
EOF

title: Egress NetworkPolicy
description:
## Egress NetworkPolicy

Egress NetworkPolicies control **outbound** traffic from pods. By default, all egress is allowed; once you apply an egress policy to a pod, only explicitly allowed egress is permitted.

**Your task:**

Create a NetworkPolicy named `restrict-egress` in the `default` namespace that:
- Applies to pods with label `role=isolated`
- Allows **egress only to pods with label `role=allowed`** on port `80`
- Blocks all other egress

hints.title: Egress NetworkPolicy structure
hints.body: Set `spec.policyTypes: [Egress]` and define `spec.egress[]` with `to` and `ports`. Omitting a type means it's not affected.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: restrict-egress
  namespace: default
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      role: isolated
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
  egress:
  - to:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          role: allowed
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 80
EOF

title: LimitRange for Default Container Limits
description:
## LimitRange

A **LimitRange** sets default resource requests/limits for containers in a namespace. Any container created without explicit limits will automatically get the LimitRange defaults.

**Your task:**

Create a namespace `bounded-ns` and a LimitRange named `default-limits` that sets:
- Default CPU limit: `200m`
- Default CPU request: `100m`
- Default Memory limit: `256Mi`
- Default Memory request: `128Mi`

hints.title: LimitRange manifest
hints.body: Use `type: Container` and the `default`/`defaultRequest` fields inside `spec.limits[]`.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: default-limits
  namespace: bounded-ns
spec:
  limits:
  - type: Container
    default:
      cpu: "200m"
      memory: "256Mi"
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: "100m"
      memory: "128Mi"
EOF

commands.command: kubectl get limitrange default-limits -n bounded-ns -o jsonpath='{.metadata.name}'
kubectl get limitrange default-limits -n bounded-ns -o jsonpath='{.spec.limits[0].default.cpu}'

title: Create a One-Shot Job
description:
## Create a One-Shot Job

A **Job** creates one or more pods and ensures a specified number of them successfully terminate. Unlike Deployments, Jobs are meant for finite tasks that run to completion.

**Your task:**

Create a Job named `pi-job` that:
- Uses `perl:5.34` image
- Runs: `perl -Mbignum=bpi -wle 'print bpi(100)'` (computes π to 100 digits)
- `completions: 1`
- `restartPolicy: Never`

hints.title: Job manifest
hints.body: Set `restartPolicy: Never` or `OnFailure` in the pod template spec (not at pod spec level).
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: pi-job
spec:
  completions: 1
  template:
    spec:
      restartPolicy: Never
      containers:
      - name: pi
        image: perl:5.34
        command: ["perl","-Mbignum=bpi","-wle","print bpi(100)"]
EOF

title: Pod Affinity vs Anti-Affinity
description:
## Pod Affinity vs Anti-Affinity

You want to ensure that **cache** pods are always scheduled on the **same node** as the **app** pods they serve (to reduce latency). Which scheduling rule should you use in the cache pod spec?

options.text: `spec.affinity.podAntiAffinity` with `requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution` matching `app=app-pod`
`spec.affinity.podAffinity` with `requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution` matching `app=app-pod`
`spec.affinity.nodeAffinity` with a `matchExpressions` for the app pod's node label
`spec.tolerations` with `key=app-pod` and `effect=NoSchedule`

explanation: **Pod Affinity** (`spec.affinity.podAffinity`) attracts pods to nodes where matching pods are already running. Use `requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution` for a hard constraint (cache pod MUST co-locate with app pod) vs `preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution` for a soft preference. **Pod Anti-Affinity** does the opposite — spreads pods apart. Tolerations are unrelated to co-location and work with taints.

hints.title: Affinity types
hints.body: podAffinity = attract to same node. podAntiAffinity = repel from same node. nodeAffinity = attract to nodes with specific labels. The `topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname` means 'same node'.
hints.command: kubectl explain pod.spec.affinity.podAffinity

title: Dynamic PVC Provisioning
description:
## Dynamic PVC Provisioning

Dynamic provisioning automatically creates a PersistentVolume when a PVC is created, using a StorageClass. k3s ships with a built-in `local-path` StorageClass.

**Your task:**

1. Create a **PersistentVolumeClaim** named `app-data` that requests:
   - Storage: `1Gi`
   - Access mode: `ReadWriteOnce`
   - StorageClass: `local-path`
2. Create a Pod named `data-pod` using `nginx:alpine` that mounts `app-data` at `/data`

```bash
kubectl get storageclass   # verify local-path exists
```

hints.title: PVC manifest
hints.body: Set `storageClassName: local-path` (k3s default). Dynamic provisioning creates the PV automatically.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: app-data
spec:
  accessModes: [ReadWriteOnce]
  storageClassName: local-path
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 1Gi
EOF

hints.title: Mount PVC in a Pod
hints.body: Reference the PVC by name in `spec.volumes[].persistentVolumeClaim.claimName`.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: data-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:alpine
    volumeMounts:
    - name: storage
      mountPath: /data
  volumes:
  - name: storage
    persistentVolumeClaim:
      claimName: app-data
EOF

title: ServiceAccount for Pod API Access
description:
## ServiceAccount for Pod API Access

ServiceAccounts provide an identity for pods that need to interact with the Kubernetes API (e.g., operators, CI runners, custom controllers).

**Your task:**

1. Create a ServiceAccount named `api-reader` in the `default` namespace
2. Create a Pod named `api-pod` using `nginx:alpine` that uses the `api-reader` ServiceAccount

```bash
# Verify:
kubectl get pod api-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.serviceAccountName}'
```

hints.title: Create a ServiceAccount
hints.body: ServiceAccounts are namespace-scoped resources. Use `kubectl create serviceaccount`.
hints.command: kubectl create serviceaccount api-reader

hints.title: Assign ServiceAccount to a Pod
hints.body: Set `spec.serviceAccountName` in the pod spec.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: api-pod
spec:
  serviceAccountName: api-reader
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:alpine
EOF

title: Cluster Upgrade Order
description:
## Cluster Upgrade Order

You need to upgrade a kubeadm-managed cluster from v1.28 to v1.29. What is the **correct order** of operations?

options.text: 1. Upgrade worker nodes → 2. Upgrade control plane → 3. Upgrade kubeadm → 4. Update kubelet/kubectl
1. Upgrade kubeadm on control plane → 2. Run `kubeadm upgrade apply` → 3. Upgrade kubelet/kubectl on control plane → 4. Drain and upgrade worker nodes one by one
1. `kubectl drain` all nodes → 2. Upgrade all nodes simultaneously → 3. `kubectl uncordon` all nodes
1. Upgrade etcd first → 2. Upgrade API server → 3. Upgrade scheduler and controller-manager → 4. Upgrade kubelet

explanation: The correct kubeadm upgrade order is: **1)** Upgrade `kubeadm` on the control plane node → **2)** Run `kubeadm upgrade apply v1.29.x` (upgrades control-plane components) → **3)** Upgrade `kubelet` and `kubectl` on the control plane, then `systemctl daemon-reload && systemctl restart kubelet` → **4)** For each worker: `kubectl drain`, upgrade `kubeadm`/`kubelet`/`kubectl`, run `kubeadm upgrade node`, restart kubelet, `kubectl uncordon`. Never upgrade workers before the control plane.

hints.title: kubeadm upgrade steps
hints.body: Key commands: `kubeadm upgrade plan` (preview), `kubeadm upgrade apply v1.29.x` (apply), `kubectl drain <node>` (before worker upgrade), `kubectl uncordon <node>` (after).
hints.command: kubeadm upgrade plan

title: Container Logging and Monitoring
description:
## Container Logging and Monitoring

A pod `worker-abc` has crashed and restarted. You want to see the logs from the **previous** (crashed) container instance, not the current one.

Which command retrieves logs from the previously crashed container?

options.text: `kubectl logs worker-abc --all-containers`
`kubectl logs worker-abc --previous`
`kubectl logs worker-abc --restart`
`kubectl describe pod worker-abc | grep log`

explanation: `kubectl logs <pod> --previous` (or `-p`) retrieves logs from the **terminated previous container** instance in a pod. This is critical for debugging CrashLoopBackOff scenarios where the container restarts before you can inspect its logs. `--all-containers` shows logs from all containers in the current run. `kubectl describe` shows events and status but not stdout/stderr logs.

hints.title: Useful kubectl logs flags
hints.body: `--previous`/`-p`: previous container | `--follow`/`-f`: stream | `--since=1h`: last hour | `--tail=50`: last 50 lines | `--timestamps`: show timestamps
hints.command: kubectl logs worker-abc --previous --tail=50

title: Create a Multi-Container Pod
description:
## Multi-Container Pods

Pods can contain multiple containers that share the same network namespace and storage volumes. This is often used for the 'sidecar' pattern.

**Your task:**

Create a Pod named `web-sidecar` in the `default` namespace with two containers:
1. Name: `app`, Image: `nginx:alpine`
2. Name: `sidecar`, Image: `busybox:1.36`, Command: `sleep 3600`

Verify it is running:
```bash
kubectl get pod web-sidecar
```

hints.title: Manifest Structure
hints.body: Your `spec.containers` array needs two items.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: web-sidecar
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:alpine
  - name: sidecar
    image: busybox:1.36
    command: ["sleep", "3600"]
EOF

commands.command: kubectl get pod web-sidecar -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[*].name}' | wc -w | tr -d ' '

title: Exposing an Application
description:
## Exposing an Application

A `redis` deployment is running, but other pods cannot reliably connect to it because pod IPs change when they restart.

**Your task:**

Create a **ClusterIP** Service named `redis-svc` to expose the `redis` deployment on port `6379`.

```bash
# Verify your service:
kubectl get svc redis-svc
```

hints.title: Use kubectl expose
hints.body: The imperative `expose` command is the fastest way to create a service for a deployment.
hints.command: kubectl expose deployment redis --name=redis-svc --port=6379 --target-port=6379

title: Labelling Resources
description:
## Labelling Resources

Labels are key-value pairs attached to objects, used to organize and select subsets of objects. A pod named `app-worker` is currently running, but it's missing a required label.

**Your task:**

Add the label `tier=frontend` to the existing pod `app-worker`.

```bash
# Verify labels
kubectl get pod app-worker --show-labels
```

hints.title: Use kubectl label
hints.body: You can label resources imperatively without editing the YAML.
hints.command: kubectl label pod app-worker tier=frontend

title: Extract Container Logs
description:
## Extract Pod Logs

A pod named `log-generator` is running and emitting log lines every second.

**Your task:**

Use `kubectl logs` to inspect the logs and confirm that the message `ERROR: Database connection failed` appears in the output.

> Tip: Use `kubectl logs log-generator | grep ERROR` to filter.

hints.title: View pod logs
hints.body: Use kubectl logs to stream the output of a running container.
hints.command: kubectl logs log-generator

hints.title: Filter logs
hints.body: Pipe kubectl logs into grep to find specific messages.
hints.command: kubectl logs log-generator | grep ERROR

setup_commands.command: kubectl run log-generator --image=busybox:1.36 --command -- sh -c 'echo "ERROR: Database connection failed" && sleep 3600'

commands.description: Pod log-generator emits ERROR: Database connection failed

title: Updating Deployments
description:
## Updating a Deployment

A deployment named `web-app` is currently running `nginx:1.24`. A security vulnerability was found in this version.

**Your task:**

Update the image of the `web-app` deployment to `nginx:1.25` to patch the vulnerability. Wait for the new pods to become ready.

```bash
# Verify your change:
kubectl describe deployment web-app | grep Image
```

hints.title: Set image command
hints.body: You can use `kubectl set image` to update a deployment's container image immediately.
hints.command: kubectl set image deployment/web-app nginx=nginx:1.25

title: JSONPath Data Extraction
description:
## Extracting Data with JSONPath

Kubectl allows you to extract specific fields from resource manifests using JSONPath, which is incredibly useful for scripting and automation.

**Your task:**

A deployment named `hidden-app` is running in the `default` namespace. Use `kubectl` with a JSONPath expression to output **only the container image name** used by that deployment.

> Expected output: `nginx:1.25.3`
