hints.command: kubectl get deployment hidden-app -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}'

commands.description: kubectl jsonpath returns the correct image

title: Generating YAML Manifests
description:
## Generating and Applying Manifests with Dry Run

Instead of writing YAML from scratch, you can use `kubectl` to generate templates using `--dry-run=client -o yaml` and then apply them.

**Your task:**

Use `kubectl run` with `--dry-run=client -o yaml` to generate a Pod manifest for `web-pod` using image `httpd:alpine`, then pipe it directly to `kubectl apply -f -` to create the pod.

```bash
kubectl run web-pod --image=httpd:alpine --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
```

hints.title: Combine dry-run with apply
hints.body: Chain kubectl run --dry-run=client -o yaml with kubectl apply -f - using a pipe.
hints.command: kubectl run web-pod --image=httpd:alpine --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

title: Bulk Deletion by Label
description:
## Bulk Deletion by Label

You can use label selectors (`-l`) to perform bulk operations on resources.

**Your task:**

There are 5 pods running in the default namespace. Delete **only** the pods that have the label `env=dev`. Leave the `env=prod` pods running.

```bash
# Verify which pods are left:
kubectl get pods --show-labels
```

hints.title: Delete with selector
hints.body: Use `kubectl delete pods` with the `-l` flag.
hints.command: kubectl delete pods -l env=dev

commands.command: kubectl get pods -l env=dev --no-headers 2>/dev/null | wc -l | tr -d ' '
kubectl get pods -l env=prod --no-headers 2>/dev/null | wc -l | tr -d ' '

title: Copy Files to Containers
description:
## Copy Files Between Containers and the Cluster

The `kubectl cp` command allows you to copy files and directories to and from running containers.

**Your task:**

A pod named `config-pod` is running with a file at `/src/config.json`. Copy it **into** the same pod at `/app/config.json` using `kubectl cp`.

> Tip: `kubectl cp` syntax is `kubectl cp <source> <pod>:<dest>`

hints.title: Use kubectl cp
hints.body: First copy the file out from /src, then copy it back to /app, or use kubectl exec to verify.
hints.command: kubectl cp config-pod:/src/config.json /tmp/config.json && kubectl cp /tmp/config.json config-pod:/app/config.json

title: Execute Commands in Pods
description:
## Execute Commands in Pods

Sometimes you need to run arbitrary commands inside a running container to debug or change state.

**Your task:**

A pod named `worker-pod` is running. Use `kubectl exec` to create an empty file at `/tmp/ready` inside the container.

```bash
# Verify the file was created:
kubectl exec worker-pod -- ls /tmp/ready
```

hints.title: Use kubectl exec
hints.body: You can pass commands to the container by adding `--` after the pod name.
hints.command: kubectl exec worker-pod -- touch /tmp/ready

title: Default Deny Network Policy
description:
## Network Policies

In a zero-trust architecture, you should deny all traffic by default and explicitly allow what is needed.

**Your task:**

Create a NetworkPolicy named `default-deny-all` in the `default` namespace that denies all ingress and egress traffic for all pods in the namespace.

```bash
# Verify your policy:
kubectl get networkpolicy default-deny-all
```

hints.title: Default Deny YAML
hints.body: Use a podSelector with an empty matchLabels `{}` to select all pods, and provide empty lists for ingress and egress.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: default
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  - Egress
EOF

commands.description: Policy applies to all pods (empty podSelector)

title: Pod Security Context
description:
## Restricting Container Privileges

Containers run as root by default, which is a major security risk.

**Your task:**

Create a Pod named `secure-pod` using the `nginx:alpine` image. Configure its security context so that it runs as user ID `1000`, runs as group ID `3000`, and sets `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false` at the container level.

```bash
# Check the security context of your pod
kubectl get pod secure-pod -o yaml
```

hints.title: Security Context Fields
hints.body: You need `securityContext` at the Pod level for `runAsUser` and `runAsGroup`, and at the Container level for `allowPrivilegeEscalation`.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-pod
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsUser: 1000
    runAsGroup: 3000
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:alpine
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
EOF

title: RBAC Least Privilege
description:
## Role-Based Access Control

Service Accounts should only be granted the permissions they explicitly need.

**Your task:**

Create a Role named `pod-reader` in the `default` namespace that only allows the verbs `get`, `list`, and `watch` on the `pods` resource. Then, bind this Role to a ServiceAccount named `read-only-sa` using a RoleBinding named `read-only-binding`.

```bash
# Verify your RBAC setup:
kubectl auth can-i list pods --as=system:serviceaccount:default:read-only-sa
```

hints.title: Imperative commands
hints.body: You can create all 3 resources using imperative commands: create serviceaccount, create role, create rolebinding.
hints.command: kubectl create sa read-only-sa && kubectl create role pod-reader --verb=get,list,watch --resource=pods && kubectl create rolebinding read-only-binding --role=pod-reader --serviceaccount=default:read-only-sa

title: Seccomp Profiles
description:
## Secure Computing Mode (Seccomp)

Seccomp restricts the system calls that a container can make to the host kernel.

**Your task:**

Create a Pod named `seccomp-pod` using the `busybox:1.36` image (command: `sleep 3600`). Configure the pod so its seccomp profile type is set to `RuntimeDefault`.

```bash
# Tip: Review PodSecurityContext documentation
```

hints.title: Set seccompProfile
hints.body: Add `seccompProfile: { type: RuntimeDefault }` to the pod's securityContext.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: seccomp-pod
spec:
  securityContext:
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault
  containers:
  - name: main
    image: busybox:1.36
    command: ["sleep", "3600"]
EOF

title: Immutable Secrets
description:
## Immutable Resources

Marking a Secret or ConfigMap as immutable protects it from accidental or malicious modifications, and also improves the performance of the kube-apiserver by significantly decreasing load.

**Your task:**

Create a Secret named `db-creds` with the key `password` and value `super-secret`. Make this secret **immutable**.

```bash
# Verify immutability:
kubectl get secret db-creds -o yaml
```

hints.title: immutable: true
hints.body: Set `immutable: true` at the root level of the Secret manifest.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: db-creds
immutable: true
stringData:
  password: super-secret
EOF

commands.description: Secret db-creds exists and is immutable

title: AppArmor Profiles
description:
## AppArmor Profiles

AppArmor is a Linux kernel security module that restricts programs' capabilities. In Kubernetes 1.30+, it is supported natively in the securityContext.

**Your task:**

Create a Pod named `apparmor-pod` using the `nginx:alpine` image. Configure its Pod-level `securityContext` to use the `RuntimeDefault` AppArmor profile.

```bash
# Verify the profile is set:
kubectl get pod apparmor-pod -o yaml | grep appArmor
```

hints.title: Set AppArmor Profile
hints.body: Add `appArmorProfile: { type: RuntimeDefault }` under `spec.securityContext`.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: apparmor-pod
spec:
  securityContext:
    appArmorProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:alpine
EOF

title: Disable ServiceAccount Token
description:
## Service Account Tokens

By default, Kubernetes automatically mounts a ServiceAccount API token into every Pod, which can be a significant security risk if the Pod is compromised.

**Your task:**

Create a Pod named `no-token-pod` using the `alpine` image (with command `sleep 3600`). Explicitly disable the automatic mounting of the ServiceAccount token for this pod.

```bash
# Verify token is not mounted:
kubectl get pod no-token-pod -o yaml | grep automount
```

hints.title: automountServiceAccountToken
hints.body: Set `automountServiceAccountToken: false` in the pod's spec.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: no-token-pod
spec:
  automountServiceAccountToken: false
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: alpine
    command: ["sleep", "3600"]
EOF

title: Block Cloud Metadata
description:
## Restricting Cloud Metadata Access

Cloud providers expose sensitive instance metadata at `169.254.169.254`. An attacker exploiting an SSRF vulnerability can use this to steal cloud credentials.

**Your task:**

Create a NetworkPolicy named `deny-metadata` in the `default` namespace that applies to all pods. It should explicitly **deny** all Egress traffic to the IP block `169.254.169.254/32`, while **allowing** all other Egress traffic.

```bash
# Tip: Use an ipBlock exception (except).
```

hints.title: Except IPBlock
hints.body: Create an Egress rule that allows all traffic (`0.0.0.0/0`) EXCEPT `169.254.169.254/32`.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-metadata
  namespace: default
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
  egress:
  - to:
    - ipBlock:
        cidr: 0.0.0.0/0
        except:
        - 169.254.169.254/32
EOF

title: Cluster-Level RBAC
description:
## Cluster Roles and Bindings

Some resources, like Nodes, are cluster-scoped and cannot be accessed using normal Roles and RoleBindings.

**Your task:**

1. Create a ServiceAccount named `monitor-sa` in the `monitoring` namespace.
2. Create a ClusterRole named `node-viewer` that grants `get`, `list`, and `watch` permissions on `nodes`.
3. Create a ClusterRoleBinding named `monitor-node-binding` to bind the ClusterRole to the ServiceAccount.

```bash
# Verify your permissions:
kubectl auth can-i list nodes --as=system:serviceaccount:monitoring:monitor-sa
```

hints.title: Imperative Commands
hints.body: Create the SA, then the ClusterRole, then the ClusterRoleBinding.
hints.command: kubectl create sa monitor-sa -n monitoring && kubectl create clusterrole node-viewer --verb=get,list,watch --resource=nodes && kubectl create clusterrolebinding monitor-node-binding --clusterrole=node-viewer --serviceaccount=monitoring:monitor-sa

setup_commands.command: kubectl create namespace monitoring --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

commands.command: kubectl get sa monitor-sa -n monitoring -o jsonpath='{.metadata.name}'

title: Pod Security Admission
description:
## Pod Security Admission (PSA)

Pod Security Admission replaced PodSecurityPolicies (PSP) to enforce security standards at the namespace level.

**Your task:**

A namespace named `secure-workloads` already exists. Add the necessary label to this namespace to **enforce** the `restricted` pod security standard.

```bash
# Verify your label:
kubectl get ns secure-workloads --show-labels
```

hints.title: Namespace Label
hints.body: Label the namespace with the `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted` key-value pair.
hints.command: kubectl label ns secure-workloads pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted

setup_commands.command: kubectl create namespace secure-workloads --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

title: Runtime Security Tools
description: Which of the following is an open-source tool specifically designed for **runtime security** in Kubernetes (e.g., detecting unexpected behavior or shell execution in running containers)?

options.text: Trivy
Kube-bench
Falco
SonarQube

explanation: Falco is a CNCF incubating project that acts as a runtime security tool. It parses Linux system calls at runtime and asserts the stream against a powerful rules engine. Trivy is for image scanning, and kube-bench checks CIS benchmarks.

title: API Server Authentication
description: To secure the `kube-apiserver`, you should prevent unauthorized users from interacting with the cluster anonymously. Which flag is used to disable anonymous authentication on the API server?

options.text: --disable-anonymous=true
--anonymous-auth=false
--auth-mode=Node,RBAC
--secure-port=6443

explanation: The `--anonymous-auth=false` flag instructs the kube-apiserver to reject any request that is not associated with a known user or service account. By default, it is enabled.

title: Minimizing Image Footprint
description: Why is it highly recommended in the CKS exam to use base images like **Alpine Linux** or **Distroless** for your containerized applications?

options.text: They automatically encrypt data at rest
They have a significantly reduced attack surface with fewer packages
They automatically configure NetworkPolicies for the pod
They guarantee that the container will not run as root

explanation: Minimal images like Alpine or Distroless contain only the bare minimum files and dependencies needed to run the application. This drastically reduces the attack surface and the number of potential CVEs.

title: Pod Security Evolution
description: PodSecurityPolicies (PSP) were completely removed from Kubernetes in version 1.25. What is the built-in native replacement that enforces Pod Security Standards at the namespace level?

options.text: NetworkPolicies
OPA Gatekeeper
Pod Security Admission (PSA)
AppArmor Profiles

explanation: Pod Security Admission (PSA) is the built-in admission controller that evaluates Pods against the predefined Pod Security Standards (Privileged, Baseline, and Restricted) based on namespace labels.

title: Securing the Kubelet
description: The kubelet exposes its own API on port 10250, which can allow an attacker to run `exec` commands on pods if left unsecured. Which configuration setting in the kubelet config file ensures unauthenticated requests are rejected?

options.text: authorization.mode: Webhook
readOnlyPort: 0
authentication.anonymous.enabled: false
protectKernelDefaults: true

explanation: Setting `authentication.anonymous.enabled: false` ensures that the kubelet will reject any API requests that do not present a valid client certificate or bearer token.

title: Read-Only Root Filesystem
description:
## Read-Only Root Filesystem

Setting `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true` prevents any process inside the container from writing to the container's root filesystem layer, significantly reducing the blast radius of a compromised container.

**Your task:**

Create a Pod named `readonly-pod` using `busybox:1.36` (command: `sleep 3600`) with:
- `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true` in the container's `securityContext`
- An `emptyDir` volume mounted at `/tmp` to provide writable scratch space

```bash
# Verify:
kubectl get pod readonly-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem}'
```

hints.title: readOnlyRootFilesystem with emptyDir
hints.body: Set `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true` in the container's `securityContext`. Mount an `emptyDir` volume at `/tmp` so the process has a writable location if needed.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: readonly-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: busybox:1.36
    command: ["sleep", "3600"]
    securityContext:
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
    volumeMounts:
    - name: tmp-vol
      mountPath: /tmp
  volumes:
  - name: tmp-vol
    emptyDir: {}
EOF

title: Drop Linux Capabilities
description:
## Drop Linux Capabilities

By default, containers are granted a set of Linux capabilities. Dropping ALL capabilities and adding back only what is explicitly needed follows the principle of least privilege and is a CKS exam requirement.

**Your task:**

Create a Pod named `nocaps-pod` using `nginx:alpine` with the following capability configuration in the container `securityContext`:
- Drop **ALL** capabilities
- Add back only **`NET_BIND_SERVICE`** (required by nginx to bind to port 80)

```bash
# Verify:
kubectl get pod nocaps-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].securityContext.capabilities}'
```

hints.title: capabilities drop and add
hints.body: Under `spec.containers[].securityContext.capabilities`, use `drop: ["ALL"]` and `add: ["NET_BIND_SERVICE"]`.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: nocaps-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:alpine
    securityContext:
      capabilities:
        drop:
        - ALL
        add:
        - NET_BIND_SERVICE
EOF

title: Secure Ingress with TLS
description:
## Secure Ingress with TLS

Exposing applications over HTTPS requires a TLS certificate stored as a Kubernetes Secret of type `kubernetes.io/tls`. The Ingress controller uses this secret to terminate TLS connections.

**Your task:**

1. A self-signed TLS certificate has been pre-generated at `/tmp/tls.crt` and `/tmp/tls.key`.
2. A deployment `tls-app` and service `tls-svc` are already running.
3. Create a **TLS Secret** named `app-tls` from the cert files.
4. Create an **Ingress** named `tls-ingress` that:
   - Routes traffic for host `secure.lab.local` to service `tls-svc` on port `80`
   - Terminates TLS using the secret `app-tls`

hints.title: Create the TLS Secret
hints.body: Use `kubectl create secret tls` with `--cert` and `--key` flags pointing to the pre-generated files.
hints.command: kubectl create secret tls app-tls --cert=/tmp/tls.crt --key=/tmp/tls.key

hints.title: Create the TLS Ingress
hints.body: Add a `spec.tls[]` block referencing the secret name alongside `spec.rules[]`.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: tls-ingress
spec:
  tls:
  - hosts:
    - secure.lab.local
    secretName: app-tls
  rules:
  - host: secure.lab.local
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: tls-svc
            port:
              number: 80
EOF

title: Private Registry Image Pull Secret
description:
## Private Registry Image Pull Secret

In production, images are stored in private registries that require authentication. Kubernetes uses `imagePullSecrets` to securely store registry credentials and inject them at image pull time.

**Your task:**

1. Create a Docker registry Secret named `registry-creds` for registry `registry.company.com` with:
   - Username: `ci-bot`
   - Password: `s3cr3t-token`
   - Email: `ci@company.com`
2. Create a Pod named `private-pod` using `nginx:alpine` that references `registry-creds` as an `imagePullSecret`

```bash
# Verify:
kubectl get pod private-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.imagePullSecrets[0].name}'
```

hints.title: Create a docker-registry Secret
hints.body: Use `kubectl create secret docker-registry` with the four required flags.
hints.command:
kubectl create secret docker-registry registry-creds \
  --docker-server=registry.company.com \
  --docker-username=ci-bot \
  --docker-password=s3cr3t-token \
  --docker-email=ci@company.com

hints.title: Reference imagePullSecrets in a Pod
hints.body: Add `spec.imagePullSecrets[].name` to the pod spec with the name of your secret.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: private-pod
spec:
  imagePullSecrets:
  - name: registry-creds
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:alpine
EOF

title: Container Runtime Sandboxing
description:
## Container Runtime Sandboxing

A security team wants to run untrusted workloads with stronger isolation than the standard `runc` runtime — specifically, they want each container's system calls to be intercepted by a user-space kernel rather than reaching the host kernel directly.

Which solution achieves this, and how is it configured in Kubernetes?

options.text: Seccomp with `RuntimeDefault` — restricts which syscalls are allowed via a BPF filter, but syscalls still reach the host kernel
AppArmor with a `deny` profile — enforces path-based MAC policies but does not intercept syscalls
gVisor (`runsc`) via a `RuntimeClass` — intercepts all syscalls in user-space with its own kernel implementation, isolating the host kernel from the container
A `PodDisruptionBudget` — prevents pod disruptions and provides workload isolation

explanation: **gVisor** (`runsc`) is a user-space kernel from Google that intercepts all container system calls before they reach the host kernel, providing strong isolation. In Kubernetes it is configured via a **RuntimeClass** resource and referenced in a pod with `spec.runtimeClassName`. Kata Containers is a similar alternative using lightweight VMs. Seccomp filters syscalls but they still hit the host kernel. AppArmor enforces file-access policies, not syscall interception.

hints.title: RuntimeClass and runtimeClassName
hints.body: Create a `RuntimeClass` that maps a name to the container runtime handler (e.g., `handler: runsc`). Reference it in a pod with `spec.runtimeClassName: <name>`.
hints.command:
kubectl explain runtimeclass
kubectl explain pod.spec.runtimeClassName

title: Kubernetes Audit Logging Levels
description:
## Kubernetes Audit Logging

You are writing an audit policy for the kube-apiserver. You need to capture the **complete request AND response body** for all write operations on `secrets` in any namespace, so you have a full record of every secret modification.

Which audit level must you specify for these events?

options.text: `None` — discard all events matching this rule; nothing is logged
`Metadata` — logs request metadata (user, verb, resource, timestamp) but not the request or response body
`Request` — logs metadata and the request body, but not the response body
`RequestResponse` — logs metadata, the full request body, and the full response body

explanation: Kubernetes audit levels in ascending verbosity: **None → Metadata → Request → RequestResponse**. `RequestResponse` is required when you need the complete picture — what was sent and what the API server returned. This is most important for sensitive resources like Secrets. The audit policy file is passed to kube-apiserver via `--audit-policy-file`. Note that `RequestResponse` is expensive; use `Metadata` for most resources.

hints.title: Audit policy levels
hints.body: The four levels: `None` (discard), `Metadata` (headers only), `Request` (+ request body), `RequestResponse` (+ response body). Configure via `--audit-policy-file` and `--audit-log-path` on the kube-apiserver.
hints.command:
# Example rule targeting Secrets at RequestResponse level:
# - level: RequestResponse
#   resources:
#   - group: ""
#     resources: ["secrets"]

title: RuntimeClass for Sandboxed Workloads
description:
## RuntimeClass for Stronger Workload Isolation

Kubernetes `RuntimeClass` lets you select an alternative container runtime (such as gVisor's `runsc`) for specific pods, providing stronger kernel-level isolation than the default `runc`.

**Your task:**

1. Create a `RuntimeClass` named `gvisor` with `handler: runsc`.
2. Create a Pod named `sandbox-pod` using the `nginx:alpine` image that references this RuntimeClass via `spec.runtimeClassName: gvisor`.

```bash
# Verify the RuntimeClass:
kubectl get runtimeclass gvisor
# Verify the pod spec:
kubectl get pod sandbox-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.runtimeClassName}'
```

> **Note:** The pod may not reach `Running` state if `runsc` is not installed on the node — that is expected in this lab. Validation only checks the API object configuration.

hints.title: Create the RuntimeClass
hints.body: Use a manifest with `apiVersion: node.k8s.io/v1`, `kind: RuntimeClass`, and a `handler` field.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: node.k8s.io/v1
kind: RuntimeClass
metadata:
  name: gvisor
handler: runsc
EOF

hints.title: Reference the RuntimeClass in a Pod
hints.body: Set `spec.runtimeClassName: gvisor` in the pod spec.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: sandbox-pod
spec:
  runtimeClassName: gvisor
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:alpine
EOF

title: Write a Kubernetes Audit Policy
description:
## Kubernetes Audit Policy

Audit logging lets you track who did what on the cluster. The kube-apiserver reads an **audit policy file** to decide which events to log and at which verbosity level.

**Your task:**

Write an audit policy file at `/etc/kubernetes/audit-policy.yaml` with the following rules (in order):

1. Log all operations on `secrets` in any namespace at level **`RequestResponse`**.
2. Log read operations (`get`, `list`, `watch`) on `pods` at level **`Metadata`**.
3. **Drop** (level `None`) all events for the `system:masters` group.
4. Log everything else at level **`Metadata`** as a catch-all.

```bash
# Verify the policy file exists and contains the key fields:
cat /etc/kubernetes/audit-policy.yaml
```

hints.title: Audit policy structure
hints.body: An audit policy is a YAML file with `apiVersion: audit.k8s.io/v1`, `kind: Policy`, and a `rules:` list. Order matters — first matching rule wins.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF > /etc/kubernetes/audit-policy.yaml
apiVersion: audit.k8s.io/v1
kind: Policy
rules:
- level: RequestResponse
  resources:
  - group: ""
    resources: ["secrets"]
- level: Metadata
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
  resources:
  - group: ""
    resources: ["pods"]
- level: None
  userGroups: ["system:masters"]
- level: Metadata
EOF

commands.description: Audit policy file is valid YAML with kind: Policy
Rule 1: level is RequestResponse and targets secrets
commands.command: python3 -c "import yaml; p=yaml.safe_load(open('/etc/kubernetes/audit-policy.yaml')); r=p['rules'][0]; print('ok' if r['level']=='RequestResponse' and any('secrets' in x.get('resources',[]) for x in r.get('resources',[])) else 'fail')"

commands.description: Rule 2: level is Metadata, targets pods, verbs include get/list/watch
commands.command: python3 -c "import yaml; p=yaml.safe_load(open('/etc/kubernetes/audit-policy.yaml')); print('ok' if any(r.get('level')=='Metadata' and any('pods' in x.get('resources',[]) for x in r.get('resources',[])) and set(r.get('verbs',[])) >= {'get','list','watch'} for r in p['rules']) else 'fail')"

commands.description: Rule 3: level is None for system:masters group
commands.command: python3 -c "import yaml; p=yaml.safe_load(open('/etc/kubernetes/audit-policy.yaml')); print('ok' if any(r.get('level')=='None' and 'system:masters' in r.get('userGroups',[]) for r in p['rules']) else 'fail')"

commands.description: Rule 4: catch-all is level Metadata with no other selectors
commands.command: python3 -c "import yaml; p=yaml.safe_load(open('/etc/kubernetes/audit-policy.yaml')); last=p['rules'][-1]; print('ok' if set(last.keys())=={'level'} and last['level']=='Metadata' else 'fail')"

title: Restrict Egress to Namespace
description:
## Namespace-Scoped Egress Network Policy

Fine-grained network policies can restrict pod traffic so that only pods within a specific namespace can communicate with each other.

**Your task:**

A namespace `frontend` and a namespace `backend` already exist. Create a NetworkPolicy named `allow-backend-only` in the `frontend` namespace that:

- Applies to **all pods** in the `frontend` namespace
- **Allows egress only** to pods in the `backend` namespace (matched by `namespaceSelector`)
- **Denies all other egress** traffic

```bash
# Verify:
kubectl get networkpolicy allow-backend-only -n frontend
```

hints.title: namespaceSelector in Egress rule
hints.body: Use `spec.policyTypes: [Egress]` with an empty egress rule except for a `namespaceSelector` that matches the `backend` namespace label.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-backend-only
  namespace: frontend
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
  egress:
  - to:
    - namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:
          kubernetes.io/metadata.name: backend
EOF

setup_commands.command: kubectl create namespace frontend --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
kubectl create namespace backend --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

commands.command: kubectl get networkpolicy allow-backend-only -n frontend -o jsonpath='{.metadata.name}'
kubectl get networkpolicy allow-backend-only -n frontend -o jsonpath='{.spec.policyTypes[0]}'

commands.description: Policy uses namespaceSelector targeting backend namespace

title: Enforce Non-Root Container
description:
## Enforce Non-Root Execution

Running containers as root is one of the most common security misconfigurations. Kubernetes provides `runAsNonRoot` and `runAsUser` to enforce this at the pod level.

**Your task:**

Create a Pod named `nonroot-pod` in the `default` namespace using the `busybox:1.36` image (command: `sleep 3600`) with the following security configuration:

- Pod-level `securityContext`:
  - `runAsNonRoot: true`
  - `runAsUser: 10001`
  - `runAsGroup: 10001`
- Container-level `securityContext`:
  - `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false`
  - `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true`
- An `emptyDir` volume mounted at `/tmp` so the process has writable scratch space.

```bash
# Verify:
kubectl get pod nonroot-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.securityContext}'
```

hints.title: Combine Pod and Container securityContext
hints.body: Put runAsNonRoot, runAsUser, and runAsGroup in the pod-level securityContext. Put allowPrivilegeEscalation and readOnlyRootFilesystem in the container-level securityContext.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: nonroot-pod
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    runAsUser: 10001
    runAsGroup: 10001
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: busybox:1.36
    command: ["sleep", "3600"]
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
    volumeMounts:
    - name: tmp-dir
      mountPath: /tmp
  volumes:
  - name: tmp-dir
    emptyDir: {}
EOF

title: Annotating Kubernetes Resources
description:
## Annotating Kubernetes Resources

Annotations attach arbitrary non-identifying metadata to Kubernetes objects — such as contact info, tool versions, or documentation URLs. Unlike labels, annotations **cannot** be used as selectors.

**Your task:**

A Deployment named `myapp` is running in the `default` namespace. Add the following annotations to it:
- `contact=ops@company.com`
- `reviewed=true`

```bash
# Verify:
kubectl get deployment myapp -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations}'
```

hints.title: kubectl annotate
hints.body: Use `kubectl annotate` to imperatively add or overwrite annotations on any resource.
hints.command: kubectl annotate deployment myapp contact=ops@company.com reviewed=true

hints.title: Overwrite an existing annotation
hints.body: If the annotation key already exists, pass `--overwrite` to update it.
hints.command: kubectl annotate deployment myapp contact=ops@company.com reviewed=true --overwrite

title: Pod with Init Container
description:
## Pod with Init Container

Init containers run **before** the main application containers start. Each init container must complete successfully before the next one begins, and all must finish before the main containers are started. They are ideal for setup tasks like seeding config files or waiting for a dependency.

**Your task:**

Create a Pod named `init-demo` with:
- An **init container** named `setup` using `busybox:1.36` that writes `ready` into `/shared/status.txt`
- A **main container** named `app` using `busybox:1.36` with command `sleep 3600`
- Both containers mount an `emptyDir` volume at `/shared`

```bash
# Verify the init container wrote the file:
kubectl exec init-demo -- cat /shared/status.txt
```

hints.title: Pod manifest with initContainers
hints.body: Define `spec.initContainers[]` above `spec.containers[]`. Both containers share the emptyDir volume mounted at /shared.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: init-demo
spec:
  initContainers:
  - name: setup
    image: busybox:1.36
    command: ["sh", "-c", "echo ready > /shared/status.txt"]
    volumeMounts:
    - name: shared-vol
      mountPath: /shared
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: busybox:1.36
    command: ["sleep", "3600"]
    volumeMounts:
    - name: shared-vol
      mountPath: /shared
  volumes:
  - name: shared-vol
    emptyDir: {}
EOF

commands.description: Init container 'setup' completed with exit code 0

title: Pod Restart Policies
description:
## Pod Restart Policies

A pod runs a one-time database migration script. If the script **fails** (non-zero exit), the container should be restarted. If it **succeeds** (exit 0), it should **not** be restarted.

Which `restartPolicy` should be set in the pod spec?

options.text: `Always` — restarts the container regardless of exit code (this is the default)
`OnFailure` — restarts only if the container exits with a non-zero code
`Never` — the container is never restarted, even on failure
`OnCompletion` — restarts the container only after it completes successfully

explanation: `OnFailure` is the right policy for batch workloads like migration scripts — Kubernetes restarts the container on any non-zero exit code but leaves it alone after a clean exit (code 0). `Always` (the default for Deployment pods) would keep restarting even after success, creating an infinite restart loop. `Never` means no automatic recovery on failure. `OnCompletion` does not exist in Kubernetes — the three valid values are `Always`, `OnFailure`, and `Never`. Jobs automatically use `OnFailure` by default.

hints.title: restartPolicy values
hints.body: Three valid values: `Always` (default for Pods), `OnFailure` (for Jobs/batch), `Never` (run-once, no retry). Check with `kubectl explain pod.spec.restartPolicy`.
hints.command: kubectl explain pod.spec.restartPolicy

title: kubectl Output Formats
description:
## kubectl Output Formats

You need to retrieve the **full live manifest** of a running deployment named `frontend` in YAML format so you can save it to a file for version control.

Which command produces the correct output?

options.text: `kubectl describe deployment frontend` — prints a human-readable summary with events
`kubectl get deployment frontend -o yaml` — prints the full manifest in YAML format
`kubectl export deployment frontend` — exports a portable manifest (removed in k8s 1.18)
`kubectl manifest deployment frontend` — a built-in command for generating manifests

explanation: `kubectl get <resource> -o yaml` (or `--output=yaml`) prints the full live manifest as YAML, including all managed fields. `kubectl describe` gives a human-readable summary with events — not a re-applicable YAML manifest. `kubectl export` was removed in Kubernetes 1.18. `kubectl manifest` does not exist. Use `-o json` for JSON output, or `-o jsonpath='...'` to extract specific fields. To save to a file: `kubectl get deployment frontend -o yaml > frontend.yaml`.

hints.title: Output format flags
hints.body: The `-o` / `--output` flag controls format: `-o yaml`, `-o json`, `-o wide` (extra columns), `-o name` (just the resource name), `-o jsonpath='...'` (field extraction).
hints.command: kubectl get deployment frontend -o yaml

title: Configure a PodDisruptionBudget
description:
## PodDisruptionBudget

A **PodDisruptionBudget (PDB)** limits the number of pods of a replicated application that are down simultaneously during voluntary disruptions (node drains, cluster upgrades). It is a critical CKA topic.

**Your task:**

A Deployment named `web-app` with **3 replicas** is already running. Create a **PodDisruptionBudget** named `web-pdb` in the `default` namespace that:
- Applies to pods with the label `app=web-app`
- Ensures **at least 2 pods** are always available (`minAvailable: 2`)

```bash
# Verify:
kubectl get pdb web-pdb
```

hints.title: PodDisruptionBudget manifest
hints.body: Use `spec.minAvailable` (integer or percentage) and `spec.selector.matchLabels` to target the right pods.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: web-pdb
spec:
  minAvailable: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web-app
EOF

hints.title: Check the PDB status
hints.body: After creating the PDB, check `kubectl get pdb web-pdb` — the ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS column shows how many pods can be disrupted at once.
hints.command: kubectl get pdb web-pdb -o wide

title: Pod Priority and PriorityClass
description:
## Pod Priority and PriorityClass

**PriorityClasses** assign a numeric priority to pods. When cluster resources are scarce, the scheduler preempts lower-priority pods to make room for higher-priority ones. This is a CKA exam topic.

**Your task:**

1. Create a **PriorityClass** named `high-priority` with:
   - `value: 1000000`
   - `globalDefault: false`
   - `description: "High priority workloads"`
2. Create a Pod named `critical-pod` using `nginx:alpine` that references the `high-priority` PriorityClass via `spec.priorityClassName`

```bash
# Verify:
kubectl get priorityclass high-priority
kubectl get pod critical-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.priorityClassName}'
```

hints.title: Create the PriorityClass
hints.body: PriorityClass is a cluster-scoped resource (not namespaced). Higher value = higher priority.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: scheduling.k8s.io/v1
kind: PriorityClass
metadata:
  name: high-priority
value: 1000000
description: "High priority workloads"
EOF

hints.title: Reference PriorityClass in a Pod
hints.body: Set `spec.priorityClassName` in the pod spec to the name of your PriorityClass.
hints.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: critical-pod
spec:
  priorityClassName: high-priority
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:alpine
EOF
